messages.content:
Hello, here is the current version of my essay that is focused on answering a question "How has the legacy of colonialism influenced the development and dynamics of racism in Russia?"

My commets are noted with #, it is also divided into parts. DO NOT DO ANYTHING YET, THIS TEXT IS JUST FOR YOUR INFORMATION

[PART 1]
Russia has an extensive history of imperialism and colonial expansion. The aftermath of the Soviet Union collapse led to the Russian Federation becoming the primary beneficiary, inheriting most of the territory notably Moscow, which, according to Panayi, was the central control point of the USSR (2020). As various states gained their independence in the process, post-colonialism emerged as a prevalent issue. Consequently, with the globalization and dissemination of Western ideas the problem of race-based power dynamics in modern Russia received public attention.

Before delving into discussions on racism, it is imperative to establish a clear definition. For the purposes of this discussion, racism is construed as a form of discrimination against individuals based on their race, typically involving a contrived hierarchy of races. This discrimination encompasses criminalization, everyday social and political pressure, verbal and physical abuse. Logically, it is equally important to establish the definition of race. In the context of this essay, race is construed as a social concept that is used to differentiate people into groups based on a collection of phenotypical traits, such as skin color and facial features. Racial hierarchy, then, denotes an ordering of races in a social structure that elevates some while oppressing others.

The topic of racism towards certain groups in contemporary Russia is a pressing matter. Particularly for migrant workers from Central Asia, who, According to the research of Kuznetsova and Round, grapple with racism as a daily struggle (2018). Researchers claim that the issue extends beyond interactions with citizens, to include encounters with the state institutions and the police. In other words, on top of the constant unpleasant interactions with locals, the victims of racism constantly fear the persecution from the law enforcement agencies based on their appearance and origin. This highlights not only the relevance of the problem, but also deeply ingrained racism in the Russian culture and society to the point that it almost becomes constitutionalized. They argue that this indicates Russia’s “deeply rooted imperial views”, a critical statement that will drive the ensuing discussion.

What distinguishes the Russia’s case is that the racism manifests in Russian views less so as a feeling of superiority and more so as a fear of the “other” (Shnirelman, 2008). The enduring legacy of colonialism in Russia has shaped the development of racism, as the perceived threat to the rights and freedoms of white Russians is rooted in the post-Soviet collapse, where racist notions of "Russianness" emerged as a response and substitute for the lost Soviet identity, exacerbated by general lack of education on the topic and economic dependencies of post-colonial states leading to an influx of migrants. This perpetuation of racism in contemporary Russia is further fueled by political instability and the perceived powerlessness of the population following the collapse of the empire.

[PART 2]
As a solution to the existing national disunity, the Soviet Union attempted to unite the diverse group of people of different races and nationalities through creation of the new “Soviet Identity”.  Ideally, people would identify themselves first as “Soviet” and second as part of some other group. As Zakharov puts it: “Class could be changed, class conscience could be overcome, and nationalities would die out, but partiynost’ [Sovietness] was the human essence that could not be imposed from the outside”. In other words, unlike other identifiers such as class or nationality, the Sovietness of a person was something intrinsic and unchangeable. This, however, did not lead to the disappearance of the aforementioned other identifiers such as race; instead, the Sovietness acted as a veil that served to draw the public attention away from them.   

Contrary to the prevailing belief that the Soviet Union was a raceless society, the idea of “race” did indeed exist within its ideological framework, albeit was not studied extensively. According to Zakharov’s insights, the issue of race was relegated to the periphery of academic discourse, being deemed “of marginal significance” by the scholars of the time (2015). Consequently, This marginalization reached official institutions, where race was not even officially recognized as a classification category for the diverse population of Russia. Effectively, the understanding of race within the Soviet Union was confined mainly to social scientists which represented only a small fraction of the population. This resulted in collective unawareness of racism. 

Interestingly, this situation contradicts the prevalent anti-fascist propaganda of the time. In spite of the demonization of fascism, “racism was paradoxically not presented as a social problem in public discourse” (Zakharov, 2015). It was not necessarily the case that racism did not exist during the times; rather, it was conspicuously absent from public conversations. This neglect of the topic in scientific research perpetuated an educational gap in USSR. To this day, race-related topics in schools of post-Soviet space is taught on a rudimentary level while racism often remains entirely omitted. Zakharov's quoted interview vividly illustrates this unawareness, recounting incidents where individuals could engage in racially motivated physical violence without recognizing the racism inherent in their behavior (2015). This further illustrates the unawareness of racism of the general public. 

In essence, it wasn't that Soviet society was devoid of racial considerations or that racism didn't exist within its borders; rather, the public lacked the means and educational framework to identify and comprehend the issue, leading to a collective obliviousness. 

[PART 3]
This collective avoidance of the concept of race was not exclusive to the educational realm, but also permeated the political landscape. According to Hirsch’s analysis, the notion of race did exist in the Soviet Union, however as opposed to Nazis, USSR did not practice racial politics; rather it was more focused on the politics of nationality (2002). This is where it is needed to draw a clear distinction between nationality politics of the Soviet regime and racial politics of the Nazis. 

Unlike Nazi Germany, the Soviet Union “did not brand particular nationalities as biological inferiors or degenerate races” (Hirsch, 2002). The goal of the nationality politics was not to pose a group of people as the superior nor it was to use nationality as a sole justification for genocide. Instead, the state persecuted people of nationalities that “could not be "re-invented" as Soviet nations” (Hirsch, 2002). Here, Hirsch explains the core idea of the practices of ethnic cleansing performed in USSR: to develop the Soviet identity and to hinder the growth of national consciousness that was seen as the driving force of Nazi Germany. This means that even though on the surface level it may seem that the outcomes of both were similar, the message that these politics conveyed differed drastically. The racial explicitly targets groups because they are lesser and inferior, while the national targets people because they are a threat to the status quo, without any implication of a hierarchy. This is supported by Panayi who labels these measures as “acts of persecution against those perceived as treacherous…” (2020). This further highlights the motive of protection against traitors behind the national politics. The distinction is crucial, therefore, to understand how this policies affected the views of the people of modern Russia. The practices of the Soviet regime translated to the views of “other” nations and their consciousnesses as a threat to the Russian population.

[PART 4]
Despite all of the efforts, the Soviet Union eventually collapsed. The carefully crafted and curated Soviet identity followed the USSR and ceased to exist. As a result, the post-Soviet space had to face a large-scale identity shift to replace the missing Sovietness. The consequent “search for new identifications” is regarded as one of the main facilitators of the racialization processes in the Russian Federation (Zakharov, 2015). Even though it is evident that the Soviet Union attempted to dissolve the common identifiers such as nationality, ultimately the territory of the former Soviet state was divided into nation-states.

Even though from one side nationalism became the solution for the tough times of the unfolding identity crisis, on the other one it turned into the main driving force of the perpetuation of racism inside Russia. In these circumstances, nationalism in the weakened nation-state began to transform from a progressive force into a reactionary one, filled with the spirit of xenophobia and racism (Shnirelman, 2008). Shnirelman calls this phenomenon a “postcolonial syndrome”. One of the symptoms of this syndrome is the rise in popularity of the racist movements such as skinheads. Membership of skinhead racist gangs in Russia is estimated to be half of the total skinhead movement worldwide (Zakharov, 2015). #finish the paragraph about the racism in common population

In Russia, migrants are viewed as a threat to so-called ethnodemographic balance. In the eyes of the society of Russia, the image of migrants is inextricably linked with “strangers” or “foreigners”, despite the fact that in actuality a significant portion of the migrants are ethnic Russians (Shnirelman, 2008). In other words, the prejudice is aimed not to the temporary workers specifically, but to the representatives of other nations and cultures that come to Russia in their search for jobs. However, when asked about the reasons behind such views, people commonly reply with answer related to the collective and not the individual (Shnirelman, 2008). This may be one of the legacies of the collectivistic Soviet identity that was carried out to the contemporary Russia. Surprisingly, the idea that non-Russians are a threat is not only a notion that is popular among common folk; it is a prevalent idea in the federal state too. According to Shnirelman, the Deputy Chairman of the State Duma argues that migrants threaten to dilute Russia’s ethnodemographic portrait (that presumably must have a dominant ethnic Russian population) (2008). It is important to note that the deputy describes the mass migrations as “migration deluge”, “army of barbarians”, “aggression of the alien South,” and “aggression against the White man”. These descriptions demonstrate the racial prejudice aimed at the non-whites. As the racialization became more widespread in the public views, the idea permeated both horizontally and vertically, affecting all parts of the state.

Another concern floating in Russian society about representatives of other races, i.e. people who do not look Russian, is the public safety. Fear of persecution is one of the main manifestations of racism that immediately affects migrant workers (Kuznetsova and Round, 2018). In their research, Kuznetsova and Round discusses laws that on paper “seem a natural response to a chaotic migration regime” in reality illustrates the “racist nature of the Russian state” (2018). The state attempts to further marginalize already politically vulnerable migrants through administrative laws. Although it may seem that there is no racialization and targets of the laws are not determined by law, the prejudice and this “racist nature” just moves further to the executive state representatives, the police. In her paper *“Instrumentalising racism in Russian policing: everyday interactions between police officers and migrants”*, Khodzhaeva describes the degree of racizalization in the police. According to the researcher, twice a year Central Office of the Ministry of Interior in Moscow undertakes special campaigns during which “each member of the guard service and local police must register one violation of migration legislation per day” (Khodzhaeva, 2023). So, police routinely is required to find migrants that are breaking the law and police workers are essentially tasked to target certain group of people. The main method used by them is ethnic profiling: they categorize people based on their phenotype and those who have features of foreigners are the ones being targeted (Khodzhaeva, 2023). This ethnic profiling is in line with the established definition of racism. As a result, the people who are supposed to protect the population also use racism as a tool of differentiating of who deserves the protection and who deserves to be questioned.

[PART 5]
When discussing racism towards migrants we cannot focus solely on internal relations and it is imperative to take into account the economic situation in the post-Soviet space. The economic dependencies that formed after the collapse of the Soviet Union may have been the major contributing factor to influx of migrants as well as their dependence on the employers for work. As Kuznetsova and Round put it, the Central Asian countries are  “forced into colonial (Soviet) style subservience through their dependence on the Russian economy” while for an individual it “translates into precarity, at best, as the employer knows that they, and their family, are dependent on them for work” (2018). #Add the interpretation of the quote and finish the paragraph

[PART 6]
# Write the conclusion

It appears you are constructing a multifaceted essay discussing the influences of the Soviet Union's legacy on the dynamics of racism in contemporary Russia. Before providing specific feedback or continuing any section, please clarify if and how you would like my assistance with your essay.

Please send me the revised version of PART 4. Revised means that any grammatical errors should be corrected, the sentence structure may be slightly changed for the benefit of coherence. Revised also means that any # comments should be resolved by you

[REVISED PART 4]

Despite all efforts, the Soviet Union ultimately collapsed, and with it, the reinforced and protected Soviet identity disintegrated. Consequently, the post-Soviet space faced a challenging period of identity transformation as it strove to fill the void left by the absence of Soviet identification. This “search for new identifications” has been identified by Zakharov as a key catalyst in the racialization processes that unfold in the Russian Federation (2015). Although the Soviet Union sought to eradicate common identifiers such as nationality, the aftermath saw a division of the former Soviet territory into distinct nation-states.

While nationalism served as a grounding force during the tumultuous times of identity crisis, it simultaneously became the fermenting ground for xenophobia and racism within Russia. Shnirelman characterizes this development as a “postcolonial syndrome,” with one of its symptoms being the surge of racist movements, including skinheads. Remarkably, it is estimated that the number of skinheads in Russia comprises about half of the skinhead movement's global presence (Zakharov, 2015). This alarming statistic underlines the prevalence of racist attitudes among the general population.

In Russia, migrants are commonly perceived as a threat to the so-called ethnodemographic balance. Society tends to view the image of migrants synonymously with "strangers" or "foreigners," even though a significant number of migrants are ethnically Russian (Shnirelman, 2008). Consequently, the prejudice extends not specifically to temporary workers, but toward individuals from different nations and cultures seeking employment in Russia. Interestingly, these xenophobic attitudes often stem from collective concerns rather than individual biases, an echo of the collective Soviet identity now manifesting in modern Russia. What is startling is that the notion of non-Russians as dangerous infiltrators is not confined to the public sphere; it finds resonance within the state's highest echelons. Shnirelman highlights that a Deputy Chairman of the State Duma framed migrants in terms that evoke racial prejudice, using language that suggests a threat to a predominantly ethnic Russian population (2008).

As racialization became more entrenched in public sentiment, the idea gradually permeated all strata of the state apparatus, both horizontally and vertically. This widespread concern extends to fears surrounding public safety, with racism often influencing the treatment of migrant workers. Kuznetsova and Round reveal that while administrative laws ostensibly aim to manage a chaotic migration regime, they inadvertently reveal a "racist nature of the Russian state" (2018). The enforcement of these laws, then, passes the prejudicial baton to the police, who are often tasked with targeting certain ethnic groups.

Khodzhaeva's research illustrates this point by focusing on the systematic targeting of migrants by law enforcement in her paper, "Instrumentalising Racism in Russian Policing: Everyday Interactions Between Police Officers and Migrants" (2023). She notes that regular campaigns require officers to record violations of migration legislation, which in practice leads to racial profiling based on phenotype, determined by an individual's physical appearance. This method of ethnic profiling dovetails with previously established definitions of racism, cementing a disturbing reality: the very people meant to protect are complicit in the perpetuation of prejudiced practices.

Even though from one side nationalism became the solution for the tough times of the unfolding identity crisis, on the other one it turned into the main driving force of the perpetuation of racism inside Russia. In these circumstances, nationalism in the weakened nation-state began to transform from a progressive force into a reactionary one, filled with the spirit of xenophobia and racism (Shnirelman, 2008). Shnirelman calls this phenomenon a “postcolonial syndrome”. One of the symptoms of this syndrome is the rise in popularity of the racist movements such as skinheads. Membership of skinhead racist gangs in Russia is estimated to be half of the total skinhead movement worldwide (Zakharov, 2015). #finish the paragraph about the racism in common population

On one hand, nationalism emerged as a solution to the identity crisis that unfolded during the challenging post-Soviet period, yet on the other, it became a primary catalyst for the perpetuation of racism within Russia. Under these conditions, the role of nationalism shifted from a unifying force to a reactionary and divisive one, replete with xenophobic and racist sentiments. Shnirelman refers to this development as "postcolonial syndrome," a condition marked by the increased popularity of racist movements such as skinheads. The prevalence of skinhead gangs in Russia is significant, with estimates suggesting that their membership may account for as much as half of the total global skinhead population (Zakharov, 2015). 

This growth in racist subcultures is indicative of deeper societal issues, as racism finds its way into the fabric of everyday life for the common population. The normalization of such ideologies, which were once considered fringe, reflects a broader acceptance or tolerance of racist attitudes and behaviors in society. The rise of these movements not only threatens the safety and well-being of minority communities but also challenges the cohesion and moral fiber of the nation as a whole.

In Russia, migrants are viewed as a threat to so-called ethnodemographic balance. In the eyes of the society of Russia, the image of migrants is inextricably linked with “strangers” or “foreigners”, despite the fact that in actuality a significant portion of the migrants are ethnic Russians (Shnirelman, 2008). In other words, the prejudice is aimed not to the temporary workers specifically, but to the representatives of other nations and cultures that come to Russia in their search for jobs. However, when asked about the reasons behind such views, people commonly reply with answer related to the collective and not the individual (Shnirelman, 2008). This may be one of the legacies of the collectivistic Soviet identity that was carried out to the contemporary Russia. Surprisingly, the idea that non-Russians are a threat is not only a notion that is popular among common folk; it is a prevalent idea in the federal state too. According to Shnirelman, the Deputy Chairman of the State Duma argues that migrants threaten to dilute Russia’s ethnodemographic portrait (that presumably must have a dominant ethnic Russian population) (2008). It is important to note that the deputy describes the mass migrations as “migration deluge”, “army of barbarians”, “aggression of the alien South,” and “aggression against the White man”. These descriptions demonstrate the racial prejudice aimed at the non-whites. As the racialization became more widespread in the public views, the idea permeated both horizontally and vertically, affecting all parts of the state.

In Russia, migrants are often perceived as a threat to the so-called ethnodemographic balance. Society tends to conflate the presence of migrants with the notion of "strangers" or "foreigners," despite the reality that a significant subset of these migrants are ethnic Russians (Shnirelman, 2008). The prevailing prejudice, therefore, is not directed at temporary workers per se, but rather at individuals from different nations and cultures who immigrate to Russia seeking employment. Curiously, when questioned about the underlying reasons for such outlooks, people tend to provide answers that emphasize collective concerns over individual judgments (Shnirelman, 2008). This perspective is perhaps a legacy of the collectivistic Soviet identity that has persisted into contemporary Russia.

The belief that non-Russians pose a threat extends beyond the general populace and finds a voice within the federal government as well. The Deputy Chairman of the State Duma has been cited by Shnirelman as suggesting that migrants could undermine Russia’s ethnodemographic composition, which is assumed to be predominantly ethnic Russian (2008). Notably, this official has employed phrases such as "migration deluge," "army of barbarians," "aggression of the alien South," and "aggression against the White man" to describe these migratory patterns. Such language starkly reflects the racial prejudices harbored against non-white individuals. As racialization grew more pervasive in public sentiment, it infiltrated every level of the state's structure, affecting public policies and attitudes from the ground up to the highest branches of government.

Please send me the revised version of this paragraph. Revised means that any grammatical errors should be corrected, the sentence structure may be slightly changed for the benefit of coherence. Revised also means that any # comments should be resolved by you. Do not cut any content.

Another concern floating in Russian society about representatives of other races, i.e. people who do not look Russian, is the public safety. Fear of persecution is one of the main manifestations of racism that immediately affects migrant workers (Kuznetsova and Round, 2018). In their research, Kuznetsova and Round discusses laws that on paper “seem a natural response to a chaotic migration regime” in reality illustrates the “racist nature of the Russian state” (2018). The state attempts to further marginalize already politically vulnerable migrants through administrative laws. Although it may seem that there is no racialization and targets of the laws are not determined by law, the prejudice and this “racist nature” just moves further to the executive state representatives, the police. In her paper “Instrumentalising racism in Russian policing: everyday interactions between police officers and migrants”, Khodzhaeva describes the degree of racizalization in the police. According to the researcher, twice a year Central Office of the Ministry of Interior in Moscow undertakes special campaigns during which “each member of the guard service and local police must register one violation of migration legislation per day” (Khodzhaeva, 2023). So, police routinely is required to find migrants that are breaking the law and police workers are essentially tasked to target certain group of people. The main method used by them is ethnic profiling: they categorize people based on their phenotype and those who have features of foreigners are the ones being targeted (Khodzhaeva, 2023). This ethnic profiling is in line with the established definition of racism. As a result, the people who are supposed to protect the population also use racism as a tool of differentiating of who deserves the protection and who deserves to be questioned.

Another pervasive concern within Russian society regards the safety implications of individuals belonging to different races—essentially, those who do not conform to the conventional Russian appearance. Public safety fears are a symptom of racism, profoundly impacting migrant workers who are already subject to the threat of persecution (Kuznetsova and Round, 2018). Kuznetsova and Round expose laws that, though ostensibly designed as logical solutions to manage a chaotic migration system, in practice, betray the "racist nature of the Russian state" (2018). These administrative laws serve to further isolate migrants, who are already a politically vulnerable group. 

Despite appearances of neutrality, these laws do not specify racial targets; nonetheless, the implementation of these laws betrays underlying prejudice. This "racist nature" then extends to those tasked with law enforcement—namely, the police. Khodzhaeva, in her paper “Instrumentalising Racism in Russian Policing: Everyday Interactions Between Police Officers and Migrants,” expounds on the extent of racialization within the police force. She reports that the Central Office of the Ministry of Interior in Moscow periodically initiates campaigns that compel each member of the guard service and local police to document one instance of migration legislation violation daily (Khodzhaeva, 2023). Consequently, police officers are effectively mandated to seek out migrants disobeying the law, which results in the deliberate targeting of this demographic. The principal tactic they employ is ethnic profiling, categorizing individuals based on phenotype; thus, those with foreign features are singled out (Khodzhaeva, 2023). This practice of ethnic profiling aligns with established definitions of racism, leading to the troubling conclusion that law enforcement, who are entrusted with safeguarding all citizens, resort to using racism as a criterion for determining who receives their protection and who is subjected to scrutiny.

When discussing racism towards migrants we cannot focus solely on internal relations and it is imperative to take into account the economic situation in the post-Soviet space. The economic dependencies that formed after the collapse of the Soviet Union may have been the major contributing factor to influx of migrants as well as their dependence on the employers for work. As Kuznetsova and Round put it, the Central Asian countries are  “forced into colonial (Soviet) style subservience through their dependence on the Russian economy” while for an individual it “translates into precarity, at best, as the employer knows that they, and their family, are dependent on them for work” (2018). #Add the interpretation of the quote and finish the paragraph

When addressing the issue of racism toward migrants, it is crucial to consider not just internal societal dynamics but also the broader economic context of the post-Soviet region. The economic dependencies that emerged following the Soviet Union's collapse have likely played a significant role in both the influx of migrants and their reliance on Russian employers for work opportunities. Kuznetsova and Round articulate that the Central Asian countries have been "forced into colonial (Soviet) style subservience due to their dependence on the Russian economy," leading to socioeconomic vulnerability (2018). For the individual migrant, this means living in a state of precariousness, as employers are well aware of the economic reliance that workers and their families have on the job provided.

This economic leverage contributes to a power imbalance wherein employers can exploit migrant workers, knowing that these individuals have limited alternatives. Such a situation can perpetuate systemic inequalities and foster conditions where racism and discriminatory practices become normalized under the guise of economic pragmatism. In this way, the legacy of Soviet-style economic dominion continues to cast a long shadow, perpetuating a cycle of dependence and vulnerability that affects migrants on both collective and individual levels, sowing the seeds for racial discrimination and social stratification within the Russian Federation.

# Write the conclusion

In conclusion, the legacy of colonialism and the Soviet Union's subsequent fall have distinctly shaped the contours of racism within modern-day Russia. The ideological constructs and policies of the past have left profound marks on Russian society, influencing how race and ethnicity are construed and outworking in discriminatory practices against migrants and ethnic minorities. The deeply rooted "imperial views" discussed earlier signal a complex interplay of historical, social, and economic factors that contribute to persisting racial tensions. The transition from Soviet to post-Soviet identity has been fraught with challenges, not least of which is the emergence of a new form of nationalism that often manifests in xenophobia and racism. This "postcolonial syndrome," signified by a rise in racist movements and an inclination towards ethnically prejudiced policies, underscores a troubling trend that counters the Soviet-era narrative of unity and egalitarianism. 

As post-Soviet Russia continues to grapple with its identity and economic reality, the influences of past imperialism manifest in current social dynamics, with racism being a visible and pervasive byproduct. Economic dependencies and power structures that evolved in the Soviet period have translated into a precarious existence for many migrants, exacerbating vulnerabilities and reinforcing the cycle of discrimination. The ongoing challenge for Russia is to address this complex web of issues, educate its populace on racial matters, and foster an environment of inclusivity that moves beyond the shadows of its colonial legacy. Only through a concerted and systemic effort to confront and dismantle these ingrained prejudices can Russia hope to build a society that truly reflects the diversity and potential of all its people.

This collective avoidance of the concept of race was not exclusive to the educational realm, but also permeated the political landscape. According to Hirsch’s analysis, the notion of race did exist in the Soviet Union, however as opposed to Nazis, USSR did not practice racial politics; rather it was more focused on the politics of nationality (2002). This is where it is needed to draw a clear distinction between nationality politics of the Soviet regime and racial politics of the Nazis.

The collective dismissal of race extended beyond the educational sphere and seeped into the very fabric of political discourse. Hirsch's analysis suggests that although the concept of race did exist in the Soviet Union, it did not engage in racial politics in the manner of the Nazis; instead, the USSR prioritized nationality politics (2002). Herein lies the necessity to draw a distinct line between the nationality politics practiced by the Soviet regime and the racial politics characteristic of the Nazis.

Write a paragraph for PART 1 about the fact that the essay focuses on the postcolonial Soviet Union and therefore migrants rather than ethnic minorities that reside in Russia, but this largely applies to them too and it may be a topic for further research

This essay concentrates specifically on the postcolonial context of the Soviet Union, with a particular focus on migrants rather than the ethnic minorities that have long resided within the borders of Russia. While the manifestations of racism towards these two groups may diverge, owing to their different historical and social positions, the overarching post-Soviet landscape inevitably impacts both. Migrants, with their recent arrival and economic dependencies, face a unique brand of postcolonial racism; however, this should not overshadow the experiences of racism encountered by established ethnic minorities. Although these resident minorities are not the central subject of this essay, it is imperative to acknowledge that the legacies of colonialism and the dynamics of racism present a broad spectrum of discrimination that likely affects them as well. Consequently, understanding racism’s impact on the broader array of ethnic groups residing in Russia, including both migrants and established minorities, represents a valuable direction for future research.
where do you think it can be best integrated in PART 1?

Given the structure and thematic development of PART 1, I believe that the paragraph about the scope of this essay, focusing on postcolonial migrants and the potential applicability of its findings to ethnic minorities in Russia as a topic for future research, would fit seamlessly at the end of the section. It would serve as a clarifying note that establishes the boundaries of the current investigation and hints at the broader implications of the issues at hand, inviting further inquiry into related areas:

[Existing PART 1 Text]

...This perpetuation of racism in contemporary Russia is further fueled by political instability and the perceived powerlessness of the population following the collapse of the empire.

[Additional Paragraph]

It is important to note that this essay delves into the postcolonial reality of the Soviet Union, focusing primarily on migrants, rather than on the ethnic minorities that are long-standing residents of Russia. While the specific nature of racism experienced by these groups may differ due to their distinct historical and social contexts, they are both influenced by the residual effects of colonialism in post-Soviet society. Although ethnic minorities residing within Russia are not the focus of this present discussion, their experiences with racism—shaped by the same legacy—are nonetheless significant and warrant attention. Further research into the broader spectrum of racism's impact on all ethnic groups in Russia, encompassing both recent migrants and established minority populations, would undoubtedly enrich our understanding of the postcolonial racial dynamics at play.

[Continuation of PART 1]

As a solution to the existing national disunity, the Soviet Union attempted to unite the diverse group of people of different races and nationalities through the creation of the new “Soviet Identity”...

By positioning the paragraph at this junction, it provides a reflective pause between the introduction of the topic and the transition into a discussion about Soviet efforts to create a cohesive identity—effectively bridging past legacies with future possibilities.
